params.text:
Give every practicable help before deciding

The second principle of the Act is clear: you must not treat someone as unable to make a decision until you have taken all practicable steps to help them, without success. Lacking capacity is a conclusion you reach last, not an assumption you start with.

Many people can make their own choices when they are given the right support at the right time. Removing that support and then deciding a person cannot decide would be both unfair and unlawful.

Practical ways to support a decision

Small adjustments often make the difference between a decision made for someone and a decision made by them.

  • Communication: use plain language, pictures, objects or a communication aid, and involve a speech and language therapist where needed.
  • Timing: choose the time of day when the person is at their best, and never rush them.
  • Accessible information: break the choice into steps and present one piece at a time.
  • Environment: find a quiet, familiar place free of distractions.
  • People: include someone the person trusts if that is what they want.

params.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the guidance on supporting people to make their own decisions.
params.textField: The second *principle* of the Act says you must take all *practicable* steps to help a person decide before treating them as unable. Using plain language and pictures supports their *communication*. Choosing the time of day when someone is most alert is about good *timing*. Deciding that a person lacks capacity should always be your *last* conclusion.
